source.title: Williams Geismar Olefins Plant Reboiler Rupture and Fire

actions_taken: Post-incident metallurgical testing of ruptured Reboiler B commissioned by CSB
Williams redesigned propylene fractionator reboilers to include pressure relief valves on shell side of each reboiler
Williams changed MOC review to a collaborative team-based process
Williams updated PHA procedure to require robust process when deviating from PHA recommendations
Williams developed new field verification requirement to ensure accuracy of P&IDs before PHA
Williams developed new definitions for standby and out-of-service equipment
Williams improved troubleshooting guidance accessible via DCS alarm right-click
Williams developed leading and lagging process safety metrics system
Williams implemented electronic tools and databases to track PHA action items and preventive maintenance
Plant remained down for 18 months and restarted in January 2015
event.summary: On June 13, 2013, at the Williams Geismar Olefins Plant in Geismar, Louisiana, a propylene fractionator reboiler (Reboiler B) that had been offline for 16 months catastrophically ruptured, causing a BLEVE and fire. The incident occurred when an operations supervisor opened the quench water (hot side) valves on the standby Reboiler B while its shell-side process valves remained closed, isolating the reboiler from its pressure relief device. Liquid propane that had accumulated in the reboiler shell during the 16-month standby period expanded thermally, generating pressure estimated between 674 and 1,212 psig, exceeding the vessel's mechanical limit. The resulting BLEVE and fire killed two Williams employees and injured 167 others. Root causes included deficiencies in Management of Change, Pre-Startup Safety Review, and Process Hazard Analysis programs spanning 12 years, as well as the absence of a hazard analysis and operating procedure for the nonroutine activities conducted on the day of the incident. The standby reboiler lacked adequate overpressure protection because the 2006 PHA recommendation to car seal open reboiler valves was only partially implemented, and no pressure relief device was installed on the reboiler shell.
recommendations: 2013-03-I-LA-R1: Implement a continual improvement program to improve process safety culture at Williams Geismar, including periodic safety culture assessments and workforce involvement.
2013-03-I-LA-R2: Develop and implement a permanent process safety metrics program tracking leading and lagging indicators covering MOC, PSSR, PHA action items, and operating procedures.
2013-03-I-LA-R3: Develop and implement a program for robust comprehensive assessments of process safety programs at Williams Geismar at least once every three years by an independent expert.
2013-03-I-LA-R4: Strengthen API Standard 521 by defining equipment operating statuses including standby and out-of-service, and specifying pressure relief requirements for each.

2013-03-I-LA-R5: Clarify API Standard 521 to require a pressure relief device for overpressure scenarios where internal vessel pressure can exceed what is allowed by the design code, including Section 4.4.12 Hydraulic Expansion.

hazards.name: Flammable liquid propane/propylene mixture under pressure in reboiler shell
Hot quench water as heat source capable of causing liquid thermal expansion in blocked-in vessel

threats.name: Liquid propane accumulation in standby reboiler shell via valve leakage or inadvertent valve opening
Introduction of hot quench water to tube side of reboiler while shell-side process valves remained closed (reboiler blocked-in)
Absence of overpressure protection on standby reboiler shell (no pressure relief device installed, no car seal on standby reboiler outlet valve)
Inadequate Management of Change: block valve installation in 2001 introduced overpressure hazard not identified or controlled
Inadequate Process Hazard Analysis: PHA action items not effectively implemented or field verified
No hazard analysis or operating procedure developed for nonroutine quench water troubleshooting activities on day of incident

consequences.name: Catastrophic rupture of Reboiler B shell
Fireball and fire lasting approximately 3.5 hours
167 reported injuries to employees and contractors
Release of over 30,000 pounds of flammable hydrocarbons
Structural damage: reboiler piping launched into pipe rack approximately 30 feet overhead

controls.name: Pressure relief valve on propylene fractionator (protecting reboilers via open path)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent overpressure of reboiler shell by providing open relief path to pressure relief device on top of propylene fractionator
controls.lod_basis: Active safeguard; pressure relief valve designed to open automatically when set pressure is reached

supporting_text: the reboiler could be isolated from its protective pressure relief valve located on top of the propylene fractionator
The shell-side process valves were closed, which isolated the shell of Reboiler B from its protective pressure relief valve on the top of the propylene fractionator
Williams did not effectively apply overpressure protection by either a pressure relief valve or by administrative controls to the standby Reboiler B

controls.name: Car seal open on shell-side outlet valve of standby reboiler (administrative overpressure protection)
controls.barrier_role: Maintain open path between standby reboiler shell and pressure relief device by locking outlet valve open with car seal
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control; car seal physically locks valve open to prevent inadvertent closure

supporting_text: only the shell-side outlet valve of the operating reboiler was car sealed open. The shell-side valves of the standby reboiler remained closed, with no car seals on the manual valves and no protective pressure relief device installed on the shell
Misunderstanding of what equipment to car seal in order to satisfy the 2006 PHA action item likely contributed to only partial completion of the action item
Car seals are 'administrative controls' that rely on human operation. They can be more prone to failure than active controls.

controls.name: Management of Change (MOC) program
controls.barrier_role: Identify and control hazards introduced by process changes before implementation in the field
controls.lod_basis: Administrative program required by OSHA PSM and EPA RMP regulations

supporting_text: Williams did not perform an MOC before installing and commissioning the new block valves on the reboilers. In fact, Williams did not perform the MOC until after the plant was operating with the new valves.
The Williams 2001 MOC review did not identify the significant overpressure hazard introduced by the valves.
Williams did not perform an MOC or a PSSR for the installation of the car seal.

controls.name: Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR) program
controls.barrier_role: Verify effective implementation of design intent, accuracy of process safety information, and proper installation and configuration of field equipment before placing equipment in service
controls.lod_basis: Administrative program required by OSHA PSM and EPA RMP regulations

supporting_text: Williams reviewers either did not answer or incorrectly answered key PSSR process safety questions.
No response was given to the question, 'PRV's [pressure relief valves] lined up and block valves car sealed open?'
The PSSR was never performed [for the car seal installation].

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) program
controls.barrier_role: Identify, evaluate, and control hazards involved in the process; issue and implement recommendations to mitigate overpressure hazards
controls.lod_basis: Administrative program required by OSHA PSM and EPA RMP regulations at least every five years

supporting_text: Williams did not sufficiently implement the recommendations issued in those PHAs and did not effectively mitigate overpressure hazards in the propylene fractionator reboilers.
The 2001 PHA team did not identify reboiler overpressure as a possible safety consequence.

Williams did not perform a field verification of the documented safeguards as part of the PHA. As a result, they did not identify the discrepancy between documentation and the actual equipment installed in the field.
The Williams PHA action item tracking system incorrectly indicated as 'complete' the 2006 recommendation to lock open at least one of the manual process valves on each reboiler.

controls.name: Operating procedures for reboiler switching and nonroutine quench water troubleshooting
controls.barrier_role: Provide clear, equipment-specific instructions for safely conducting reboiler switching and quench water troubleshooting activities, including correct valve sequencing and overpressure hazard awareness
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control; written procedures required by OSHA PSM

supporting_text: Williams did not conduct a hazard analysis and develop a procedure for the operations activity.

Williams had not equipped this reboiler with a protective pressure relief device. Williams considered this generic procedure applicable to start up the propylene fractionator Reboiler B; however, Williams' generic procedure was based on the assumption that all reboilers had the process fluid on the tube side of the reboiler, which was not the configuration of the propylene fractionator Reboiler B.
Williams did not create a procedure specifically for switching the propylene fractionator reboilers.

controls.name: Instrumentation to detect process fluid accumulation in standby reboiler shell
controls.barrier_role: Detect presence of liquid propane in standby reboiler shell to alert operators before hot quench water is introduced
controls.lod_basis: Engineering detection control; pressure gauge or level instrumentation on reboiler shell

supporting_text: Williams had not installed instrumentation to detect process fluid within the reboiler. As a result, Williams personnel did not know that the standby Reboiler B contained liquid propane.

Reboiler B did not have a pressure gauge installed on its shell to allow for periodic monitoring. A pressure gauge could have alerted the operations supervisor that the Reboiler B shell was at a pressure of at least 124 psig.

controls.name: Robust isolation of standby reboiler using blinds (positive isolation)
controls.barrier_role: Prevent process fluid from entering standby reboiler shell during offline period by using blind flanges rather than relying solely on block valves
controls.lod_basis: Engineering passive control; blind provides positive isolation superior to block valves

supporting_text: Closed gate (block) valves leak, and they are susceptible to inadvertent opening. Both scenarios can introduce process fluids to offline equipment. More robust isolation methods, such as inserting a blind, can better protect offline equipment from accumulation of process fluid.
The propane could have entered the standby reboiler via a mistakenly opened valve, leaking block valve(s), or another unknown mechanism.

controls.name: Relief valve engineering analysis and action item follow-up
controls.barrier_role: Identify inadequate overpressure protection through contracted engineering analysis and ensure corrective actions are implemented
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control; contracted engineering review with action item tracking

supporting_text: Williams contracted an engineering services firm to perform a relief valve engineering analysis of the Williams Geismar facility in 2008 to ensure the valves were properly sized for the equipment they were designed to protect. The analysis identified that the propylene fractionator reboilers did not have sufficient overpressure protection.
The CSB learned that Williams did not develop an action item to address this relief valve engineering analysis for the propylene fractionator reboilers.

controls.name: Emergency response and firefighting
controls.barrier_role: Control and extinguish fire following BLEVE to limit escalation and protect personnel
controls.lod_basis: Emergency response; fire lasted approximately 3.5 hours before being controlled

supporting_text: The fire lasted approximately 3.5 hours

controls.name: Post-incident installation of pressure relief valves on shell side of each reboiler
controls.barrier_role: Provide direct active overpressure protection on reboiler shell to prevent future overpressure events
controls.lod_basis: Active engineering safeguard; pressure relief valve installed directly on reboiler shell post-incident

supporting_text: Following the June 13, 2013 incident, Williams redesigned the propylene fractionator reboilers to include a pressure relief valve on the shell side of each reboiler.

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
